critique.summary: The UK regulator Ofcom fined the US messaging platform 4Chan £520,000 for breaches of the Online Safety Act, including failure to implement age checks and risk assessments. 4Chan has mocked the fine and stated it will not pay, arguing that its conduct is legal in the US and protected by the First Amendment.
mainPoints: Ofcom fined 4Chan £520,000 for failing to comply with the UK's Online Safety Act.
The fines include £450,000 for lack of age checks to prevent children from accessing pornography.
Additional fines of £50,000 and £20,000 were imposed for failing to assess risks and protect users from criminal content.
4Chan's lawyer responded with an AI-generated cartoon and stated the platform is not breaking US law.
Ofcom emphasized the importance of protecting children online and enforcing age checks and risk assessments.
4Chan has refused to pay previous fines from Ofcom.
Ofcom has issued nearly £3 million in fines to various tech companies under the Online Safety Act, with limited payment compliance.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with publicly available information about Ofcom's enforcement of the UK's Online Safety Act and the fines issued to 4Chan. The amounts and reasons for the fines align with official statements from Ofcom. The response from 4Chan's lawyer and the platform's stance on the fines have been reported by multiple reputable sources. The article also accurately reflects Ofcom's broader enforcement actions and challenges in collecting fines. Overall, the article is factually accurate and truthful.
considerations: The article does not explore the legal complexities of jurisdiction and enforcement of UK laws on US-based companies like 4Chan.
There is no detailed discussion of the effectiveness of the Online Safety Act or potential impacts on free speech.
The article does not provide perspectives from 4Chan users or independent experts on online safety regulations.
The long-term implications for international tech companies facing UK regulations are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the UK's attempts to regulate online content, particularly focusing on 4Chan and the implications of such regulations on free speech and international law. Many commenters express skepticism about the effectiveness and legality of these measures.

comments.author: dijit
comments.text:
The response from Ofcom doesn’t stand up to scrutiny.

If you are to sell a toy in the UK you must be a British company. (and must pay VAT and comply with British safety standards).

If a consumer buys from overseas and imports a product then they do not have British consumer protections. Which is why so much aliexpress electrical stuff is dangerous (expecially USB chargers) yet it continues to be legally imported.

Just, no british retailer would be allowed to carry it without getting a fine.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a clear and logical critique of Ofcom's stance, highlighting inconsistencies in regulatory practices.

comments.author: kimixa
comments.text:
People here seem to be thinking this a UK/Europe-specific phenomenon, but there's plenty of examples of the US "seizing" sites that were never hosted in the USA either, and even put pressure on countries to extradite people involved even if they never broke any laws in the country they're living in.

One I remember was a site hosting streams of the 2022 football world cup. Or a number of Iranian-affiliated news sites just last year. Or offshore gambling websites in 2021.

People going "Those Crazy Brits! Thank God That'll Never Happen Here!" seem pretty ill-informed.
comments.analysis: The comment effectively broadens the discussion by providing historical context, suggesting that similar actions have occurred in other jurisdictions.

comments.author: ecshafer
comments.text: UK fining an American company for this is absurd. 4Chan isn't breaking any laws. You can make it illegal for your own citizens but you can't regulate a foreign business. UK citizens should fight for the right to free speech though.
comments.analysis: This comment succinctly captures the absurdity of the situation, questioning the jurisdictional authority of the UK over a foreign entity.

comments.author: windowliker
comments.text:
4chan is already on most of the adult content filters used in the UK at ISP level (along with other such egregious offenders against childhood purity as archive.org) meaning that an explicit request to remove the filter is often required to access it... That it has taken this long to implement a universal block at the ISP level shows that the motive is something other than 'protecting the innocent'.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_blocking_in_the_United_Kin...
comments.analysis: The commenter raises valid concerns about the underlying motives of the regulations, suggesting a deeper agenda beyond child protection.

comments.author: rconti
comments.text:
> "Companies – wherever they're based – are not allowed to sell unsafe toys to children in the UK. And society has long protected youngsters from things like alcohol, smoking and gambling. The digital world should be no different," she said.

So the UK plans to fine Parisian bars that serve alcohol to British under-18s in France on holiday?
comments.analysis: This comment uses humor to critique the absurdity of the UK's regulatory reach, although it may detract from a serious discussion.

comments.author: mgaunard
comments.text:
The age check laws is just funneling money directly to VPN companies.

There's no way anyone sensible would give over their identity to dodgy websites. It's easier to just pretend to be in a different country.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a potential unintended consequence of the regulations, suggesting they may lead to increased use of VPNs.
